title: A multivariate correlated poisson generalized inverse gaussian regression model for dependent count data: Estimation and testing procedures
author: Hanike, Purhadi, et al.

steps.title: Methodological details
steps.instruction: Parameter estimation and hypothesis testing for MCPGIGR begin with formulation of the multivariate Poisson and GIG, which mixes a multivariate Poisson distribution with a GIG random effect to induce dependence and handle overdispersion. Building on this distribution, we specify the MCPGIG regression with a log link and involve exposure variable. Parameters are estimated by MLE using the BHHH algorithm, and statistical tests are derived using MLRT for joint-parameter test and Wald Z-test for individual test. Detailed derivations are presented in Appendix A.

steps.title: Hypothesis testing procedures for the mcpgigr model
steps.instruction: We use MLRT for simultaneous hypotheses and Wald Z tests for partial (coefficient-wise) inference.

steps.title: Hypothesis testing procedures for the mcpgigr model
steps.instruction: Partial tests. If the simultaneous hypothesis test rejects H0, then testing continues with a partial test to determine which predictor variables individually influence the response variable. The hypothesis for the partial test is as follows:

steps.title: Hypothesis testing procedures for the mcpgigr model
Hypothesis testing procedures for the mcpgigr model
steps.instruction: The asymptotic normality of the MLE is not derived in detail here but follows from the general asymptotic theory of MLE under standard regularity conditions [34]. The test statistic ZMCPGIGR is the statistic asymptotically standard normal. Thus, The test statistic used is:(13)ZMCPGIGR=خ²^jkSE(خ²^jk)âˆ¼nâ†’âˆ‍N(0,1)

steps.title: Hypothesis testing procedures for the mcpgigr model
steps.instruction: The rejection region for H0 is when the |ZMCPGIGR|>Zخ±/2 where خ± is the significance level. imultaneous hypotheses are evaluated using a likelihood-ratio test (comparing the unrestricted and restricted models), whereas individual predictor effects are assessed with Wald Z-statistics based on the asymptotic MLE variance (from the outer product of gradients or the observed Hessian). Together, these procedures provide a coherent testing framework for multivariate responses.
